title: Beyond Awareness: Why Nigeria Must Fund a Period-Friendly Future
content_text: Every year on 28 May, Nigeria joins the global community to observe Menstrual Hygiene Day and its goal of building a #PeriodFriendlyWorld , where menstruation does not limit dignity, health, education, or opportunities. In 2025, the global campaign reached 971 million people , demonstrating how menstrual health has shifted from silence to a widespread public discussion. But visibility has not yet translated into access. Across Nigeria, too many girls still lack the basics they need to manage their periods safely and with dignity. Menstrual products are too expensive for many families, and even when they have them, schools may not have clean or private toilets, no water or soap and nowhere to throw used pads away. Many girls also don’t have accurate information about their bodies, or an adult they can ask without embarrassment. This is what period poverty looks like; not only a lack of awareness, but many gaps at once in products, facilities, information, and support. Girls display pads and period-friendly kits, reflecting how dignity improves when information is paired with practical, monthly menstrual support systems. Image credit: Nigeria Health Watch What girls’ lived experiences teach us about period poverty To mark Menstrual Hygiene Day 2026, Nigeria Health Watch, in collaboration with NoGirlWithoutAPad , visited City Royal School in Abuja for an outreach programme centred on menstrual health awareness, education, and support. Fifty students, comprising 30 girls and 20 boys aged 14 to 16, engaged in discussions about menstrual hygiene management, myths, misconceptions, school attendance, confidence, and the support young people need to speak openly about menstruation without shame. The session revealed how much fear and misinformation girls still manage in silence. “Can disposed sanitary pads be used for spiritual purposes?” a girl asked, explaining that stories she had heard made her afraid to throw used pads into the bin. Her question was not simply about disposal. It showed how stigma, fear, myths, and misinformation can influence the way girls experience their periods. Several girls also explained that there were no sanitary pads available at school and that the toilets sometimes lacked water, making it difficult to manage menstruation during school hours. Another girl described starting her period at school without a pad and without water in the toilet. She had to leave school. When she got home, her parents were not there, so she asked a neighbour for money to buy a pad and later had to repay it herself. Reflecting on her experience, she called for increased compassion from teachers and parents. “Teachers should be more understanding rather than saying, ‘Don’t they know they are getting their period today?’ and parents should be more observant and supportive of their children’s menstrual health needs,” she said. A student speaks during the discussion, reminding stakeholders that girls need safe spaces to ask questions without fear or embarrassment. Image credit: Nigeria Health Watch These interactions raise an important question. Is Nigeria’s period poverty mainly caused by limited menstrual awareness, or is it rooted in deeper systemic failures? Awareness is important, but awareness alone cannot end period poverty. A girl may know how to manage her period safely and still miss school if she cannot afford sanitary pads, if her school toilet lacks water or privacy, if there is nowhere to safely dispose of used products, or if she fears being teased for staining her uniform. The World Bank describes menstrual health and hygiene as a multi-sector issue that requires water, sanitation, and hygiene (WASH) facilities, affordable materials, accurate information, and a supportive environment free of stigma. Why period poverty is an infrastructure and affordability crisis For many girls in Nigeria, menstruation still interrupts education because the school environment is not designed to support menstrual health. Only 11% of schools have gender-sensitive basic WASH services; only 8% have separate girls’ toilet compartments with facilities for menstrual hygiene management; and only about 3% have basic gender-sensitive WASH services. These numbers show that the problem is not only about personal knowledge, but also involves the lack of essential infrastructure. Image credit: Nigeria Health Watch Period poverty is a public health, education, WASH, gender equity, and human rights issue. In Nigeria, it is also closely linked to poverty. 63% of the population in Nigeria, approximately 133 million people, are multidimensionally poor. Poor households in Nigeria spend up to 70% of their income on food. For families already struggling to afford food, sanitary pads can quickly become a monthly expense that is delayed, rationed, or cut. The high cost of sanitary products became clear when a girl asked whether disposable pads could be washed and reused. That question, “Can I wash and reuse a disposable pad?” , revealed how unaffordable menstrual products are for some students. This is why period poverty cannot be solved by one-off pad donations alone, even though donations provide immediate and necessary relief. Menstruation is not a one-day event; it happens every month. A period-friendly Nigeria, therefore, needs systems that make menstrual products affordable, accessible, safe, and normal in schools, homes, workplaces, health facilities, and public spaces. Awareness has increased, but access has not kept up Across Nigeria, civil society organisations, schools, humanitarian actors, and health advocates have worked for years to raise awareness of menstrual health and reduce stigma. These efforts matter because they create safer conversations, reduce shame, and provide immediate support. In conflict-affected communities, the International Committee of the Red Cross (ICRC) and the Nigerian Red Cross Society have trained over 800 adolescent girls in IDP settlements and camps in Adamawa and southern Bo
summary: The post discusses period poverty in Nigeria, highlighting the lack of access to menstrual products, clean and private toilets, and accurate information about menstrual health. It reports on a menstrual health awareness program at City Royal School in Abuja, where students shared their experiences with period poverty.

title: Community-Led Assessment Reveals Priorities for Improved Healthcare Services in Kano
content_text: Rahima Shehu Dokaji Can a health facility truly meet the needs of its community when residents are unaware of available health insurance benefits and health workers are stretched beyond their capacity? These concerns dominated discussions during a town hall meeting at Zubairiya Primary Healthcare Centre (PHC) in Yakasai community, Kano State. The town hall meeting and community scorecard assessment, organised by Nigeria Health Watch in collaboration with Akin Savvy Awareness and the Kano State Primary Healthcare Management Board, brought together community members, health workers, Ward Development Committee (WDC) members, traditional leaders, and government representatives to review healthcare service delivery and the implementation of the Basic Health Care Provision Fund (BHCPF) at the facility. Community leaders present at the town hall meeting. Image credit: Nigeria Health Watch During the meeting, residents expressed concerns about inadequate staffing, poor awareness of health insurance enrolment, lack of awareness of the existence of WDC and its functions, and limited infrastructure, which were also reflected in the scorecard assessment. Participants noted that many community members registered under the BHCPF were unaware of their enrolment status and the benefits available to them. Scorecard assessment result for the Zubairiyya PHC translated in English and pasted in the Health facility. Image credit: Nigeria Health Watch A representative of the ward head, Muhammad Auwal Yakasai, acknowledged the facility s importance to the community, saying, “we are happy with the facility because we use it.” Other residents, including Zainab Ali Usman and Khadija Abdul Yakasai, commended health workers for maintaining cordial relationships with patients and reducing waiting times despite existing challenges. The town hall also revealed concerns about inclusion and infrastructure. During the community scorecard session, stakeholders observed that no representative of Persons with Disabilities (PWDs) was present, despite their expected representation within the WDC structure. Scorecard assessment result for the Zubairiyya PHC translated in hausa and pasted in the Health facility. Image credit: Nigeria Health Watch Community members further raised concerns about the absence of a functional patient toilet. A WDC member explained that the facility’s existing toilet had been converted into a delivery room to address urgent maternal healthcare needs, while funding constraints had delayed plans to construct a temporary replacement. Providing expert insights, the Officer-in-Charge of the facility, Musa Ibrahim Buhari, stated that BHCPF enrolment had improved significantly . “People are now coming daily to enrol, unlike before. We also provide family planning, immunisation, and other essential healthcare services,” he said. However, he acknowledged that staffing shortages continue to affect service delivery. Female participants from the community seated during the town hall meeting. Image credit: Nigeria Health Watch The discussions highlighted how inadequate human resources, infrastructure deficits, and limited awareness of health insurance services can reduce the effectiveness of BHCPF interventions. While residents acknowledged improvements in service delivery, stakeholders agreed that addressing these gaps is critical to achieving better health outcomes. These findings support the objectives of Nigeria’s Health Sector Renewal Strategic Plan , particularly its commitment to strengthening primary healthcare systems, improving healthcare financing, and ensuring equitable access to quality services for all citizens. More participants at the town hall meeting. Image credit: Nigeria Health Watch Therefore, community leaders, government agencies, and development partners should prioritise; Investments in health worker recruitment, Inclusion of People Living with Disabilities in health dialogues Community sensitisation and awareness campaigns on existing accountability mechanisms and health insurance coverage, and Construction of a functional toilet facility. As discussions concluded, participants agreed that regular town hall meetings remain vital for promoting accountability, amplifying community voices, and ensuring that healthcare services respond to the needs of the people they serve.
summary: A community-led assessment in Kano State revealed concerns about inadequate staffing, poor awareness of health insurance enrolment, lack of awareness of the existence of Ward Development Committee and its functions, and limited infrastructure at the Zubairiya Primary Healthcare Centre. Residents expressed concerns about the absence of a functional patient toilet and the need for inclusion of People Living with Disabilities in health dialogues.

title: The Anatomy of an Outbreak in a Conflict Setting Nigeria’s Preparedness for Ebola
content_text: The latest Ebola outbreak in DRC is unfolding in a conflict-affected region where insecurity, displacement, and fragile trust are complicating containment efforts. What does this reveal about Nigeria s preparedness for outbreaks in its own most challenging settings? When the World Health Organization (WHO) declared the Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of Congo and Uganda a Public Health Emergency of International Concern, it was issuing the highest level of alarm available under the International Health Regulations for the ninth time in history, the third involving Ebola, and the first for an outbreak caused by the Bundibugyo strain , for which no licensed vaccine exists. The declaration reflected growing concern about an outbreak unfolding in a conflict-affected region where insecurity, displacement, and fragile community trust are making containment significantly harder . As WHO Director-General Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus stated at the recent media briefing on the Ebola outbreak: “The key to ending this outbreak is not biomedical. It’s leadership, ownership, partnership and trust.” Nigeria responded with familiar and necessary measures, activating an Emergency Operations Centre (EOC), designating laboratories, and completing a risk assessment, placing national preparedness at 59%. These essential steps are a standard minimum response that most countries with functional public health systems would be expected to mount, but they answer the easier question. Image credit: Nigeria Health Watch The harder question is what happens if Nigeria’s potential index case does not arrive at an international airport, as in 2014, but instead appears in one of the country’s conflict-affected communities, where years of insecurity have weakened healthcare infrastructure and institutional trust? Nigeria is not starting from zero Nigeria’s track record of responding to concurrent outbreaks justifies its preparedness measures. A swift response contained the 2014 Ebola outbreak within weeks through rapid contact tracing, a unified command, and political leadership, lessons that shaped how Nigeria approached subsequent outbreaks. Years of Lassa fever surveillance coordinated through NCDC’s National Reference Laboratory strengthened diagnostic systems that would prove critical during COVID-19, when the NCDC built directly on that foundation to achieve a record expansion of testing laboratories, train more than 35,500 health workers on infection prevention, and develop genomic sequencing capacity now extended across multiple pathogens. More recently, investments in clinical trial readiness for Lassa fever across high-burden states are beginning to build the institutional research infrastructure that could underpin epidemic preparedness. At a recent emergency meeting convened by the House of Representatives Committee on Healthcare Services, which Nigeria Health Watch attended, NCDC Director General Dr Jide Idris updated the House on Nigeria’s preparedness for Ebola. Emergency Meeting on Ebola Preparedness at the Federal House of Assembly captured in the photo include representatives from WHO (Nigeria Country Representative Head of Mission); NCDC (Director General key representatives); Nigeria Immigration Service; Nigeria Customs Service; Nigeria Health Watch; Vaccine Network and LISDEL. Image credit: Ifeoluwa Ojo/NCDC In a subsequent Arise TV interview , he was direct: “I’ll be frank with you, we are not 100% ready, but we are improving our readiness. With a new Ebola outbreak now declared, a critical question is how much of the infrastructure built during the 2014 Ebola response and the COVID-19 pandemic has been sustained well enough to mount a robust response today . The realities shaping Nigeria’s outbreak readiness An outbreak risk assessment evaluates multiple pillars of preparedness, from the likelihood of importation and spread, to capacity for prompt diagnosis, surveillance at points of entry, and health workforce readiness. The outbreak in the DRC shows that preparedness depends on how those pillars perform under conditions of insecurity, population displacement and weak institutional trust. Nigeria faces multiple security challenges across different parts of the country. Fifteen years into the northeast crisis , approximately 2.3 million people have been internally displaced across Borno, Adamawa, and Yobe, with 5.9 million requiring humanitarian assistance and 31% of health facilities out of operation during recent crisis periods. Beyond the northeast insurgency, Nigeria has been ranked the fifth most dangerous country globally in December 2024, with around 15,000 kidnappings recorded between 2019 and 2025. These same conditions of insecurity and displacement are also making it harder to contain the outbreak in eastern DRC, where the WHO says contact tracing coverage must increase from 45% to more 90% to bring transmission under control. The trust deficit runs deeper still. During the COVID-19 outbreak, research found that 56.8% of Nigerians surveyed mistrusted the government, and this mistrust was directly associated with lower vaccine acceptance, a pattern confirmed across multiple studies. In the event of a potential Ebola response, where isolation and contact tracing are the primary containment tools, that trust deficit could make it harder to control the outbreak. Preparedness is built long before the crisis As Dr Chikwe Ihekweazu, Executive Director of the WHO Health Emergencies Programme, observed , “To build trust in the people you serve, you have to be there for them all the time. Whether it’s a woman giving birth, a child brought for immunisation, or treatment for malaria, if you’re not able to solve their day-to-day problems, why do you expect them to welcome you when there’s an Ebola outbreak?” Image credit: Nigeria Health Watch Dr Abdi Mahamud, WHO Director for Health Emergency Alert and Response Operations, drew a similar connection when he said: “What is critically differen
summary: Nigeria's preparedness for Ebola outbreaks in conflict settings is being assessed in light of the ongoing outbreak in the Democratic Republic of Congo. Nigeria has a track record of responding to concurrent outbreaks, including the 2014 Ebola outbreak, and has built infrastructure that could underpin epidemic preparedness. However, the country is not 100% ready for an outbreak, and a critical question is how much of the infrastructure built during the 2014 Ebola response and the COVID-19 pandemic has been sustained.

title: An update of Lassa fever outbreak in Nigeria (Week 22)
content_text:
Lassa Fever Situation Report                                                  Epi Week: 23 2026 
1 | Page  
			Nigeria Centre for Disease Control and Prevention              Protecting the health of Nigerians  www.ncdc.gov.ng         PLOT 801 EBITU UKIWE STREET, JABI ABUJA, NIGERIA.          TOLL-FREE CALL: 6232. Email: info@ncdc.gov.ng                @NCDC.gov.ng

title: In Kaduna, Saminaka PHC Brings Quality Care Closer to Rural Residents
content_text: Community members and health workers report notable improvements in infrastructure, staffing, sanitation, and service delivery in Saminaka Primary Healthcare Centre (PHC), located in Lere Local Government Area (LGA) of Kaduna State, under the IMPACT Project . According to facility officials, the renovation included repainting of buildings, construction of a security post, improved water supply, upgraded toilet facilities, and installation of an incinerator for waste management. The facility also now receives drugs directly from the Kaduna State Contributory Health Management Authority ( KADCHMA ). For Halima, a resident of Saminaka, the changes at the PHC have restored confidence in the health system. Since the renovation, we have been very happy, she said . More people now come to the hospital because it s clean, everything has been repaired, and the staff are available. They attend to us much faster than before. Even relatives of patients are always happy when they see how hygienic the environment is . The installed solar system powering the Saminaka comprehensive health facility. Image credit: Nigeria Health Watch Her experience reflects the views of many community members who now see the facility as a place where they can receive timely and quality care. The Facility Manager, Mr Theophilus Iliya, noted that the revitalisation has improved working conditions and service delivery. We are enjoying the facility more than before, he explained . The building was repainted, a security room was constructed, and more staff were added across all departments . Newly constructed canopy at the facility to accomodate more women during immunisation and antenatal visits. Nigeria Health Watch Usman Jaafar, the Health Information Officer, highlights improvements in infrastructure and staffing. We now have access to clean running water in every unit of the hospital , he stated. The toilets were upgraded to a water-flush system, and an incinerator was installed to ensure proper waste disposal . According to Jaafar, staffing increased from 11 to about 21 permanent and functional staff following the revitalisation, contributing to improved service delivery and increased patient turnout. Although the facility has recorded significant improvements, challenges remain. The Ward Development Committee (WDC) Chairman, Hussaini Abubakar, notes that the facility depends heavily on volunteers who have served for years without permanent employment. According to Hussaini, many volunteers have worked at the facility for more than a decade, filling essential roles without job security. Inside the female ward of the health facility admitted with patients. Image credit: Nigeria Health Watch Another challenge is the absence of staff accommodation. Health workers commute from their homes, limiting their availability during emergencies and night shifts. The lack of an ambulance further complicates emergency referrals. The facility manager, noted that staff often rely on personal vehicles or commercial tricycles to transport patients requiring higher-level care, sometimes causing delays in urgent situations. The improvements at Saminaka PHC highlight the progress made through revitalisation investments in primary healthcare infrastructure and workforce development, which is part of the Nigeria Health Sector Renewal Investment Initiative . While there are still some gaps to address, tackling these issues will lead to more comprehensive care for the rural population. Waiting area at the health facility. Image credit: Nigeria Health Watch The Kaduna State Government and relevant stakeholders should prioritise; Strengthening workforce support through recruitment and regularisation of long-serving volunteers Provide staff accommodation Improve emergency referral systems through ambulance provision
location: Saminaka PHC, Lere Local Government Area, Kaduna State
summary: The Saminaka Primary Healthcare Centre in Kaduna State has undergone renovations, including repainting of buildings, construction of a security post, improved water supply, upgraded toilet facilities, and installation of an incinerator for waste management. The facility now receives drugs directly from the Kaduna State Contributory Health Management Authority. Community members report notable improvements in infrastructure, staffing, sanitation, and service delivery.

title: Engagement of Consultant for the Assessment and Appraisal of the Nigerian Business Coalition Against AIDS (NiBUCAA) and the HIV Trust Fund Nigeria (HTFN)
content_text: NATIONAL AGENCY FOR THE CONTROL OF AIDS (NACA) REQUEST FOR PROPOSALS Engagement of Consultant for the Assessment and Appraisal of the Nigerian Business Coalition Against AIDS (NiBUCAA) and the HIV Trust Fund Nigeria (HTFN) May 2026 Letter of Invitation Dear Sir/Ma: The National Agency for the Control of AIDS (NACA) invites proposals for consulting services for the following assignment Engagement of Consultant for the Assessment and Appraisal of the Nigerian Business Coalition Against AIDS (NiBUCAA) and the HIV Trust Fund Nigeria (HTFN) The primary objective of this assignment is to Review NiBUCAA and the HTFN’s governance structure and institutional arrangements. Evaluate operational efficiency and programmatic performance since inception. Assess financial sustainability and private sector engagement mechanisms. Review policy alignment with national HIV and health financing strategies. Develop actionable recommendations and a restructuring roadmap. The National Agency for the Control of AIDS (NACA) will make available the necessary documents and reports and other information concerning this assignment. You may obtain additional information at the following address: National Agency for the Control of AIDS (NACA) Ground Floor No. 3 Zinguinchor Street, Beside AEDC Office, Wuse zone 4, Abuja. A candidate will be selected on the basis of comparison of Budget presented by the candidates, individual qualifications (Curriculum Vitae and Experience), Technical Approach, Methodology and Workplan The following forms are attached: ( a ) Terms of Reference; ( b ) Model of Curriculum Vitae; ( c ) Model of Letter for submitting the Proposal; ( d ) Form for Observations of the Consultant on the Terms of Reference; Yours sincerely, Clement Afolabi Ag.Head Procurement For Director General Engagement of Consultant for the Assessment and Appraisal of the Nigerian Business Coalition Against AIDS (NiBUCAA) and the HIV Trust Fund Nigeria (HTFN) Background and Context The Nigerian Business Coalition Against AIDS (NiBUCAA) and HIV Trust Fund of Nigeria was established as a private sector platform to mobilise resources, coordinate workplace and community-based HIV prevention, care, and support interventions in Nigeria. The platforms was designed to complement the efforts of the National Agency for the Control of AIDS (NACA) by mobilizing corporate leadership, resources, and advocacy support for the national HIV response. Over time, NiBUCAA and HTFN’soperational effectiveness, private sector engagement, and institutional visibility have declined. With Nigeria’s increasing focus on domestic resource mobilization and sustainability of the HIV response, there is a critical need to assess NiBUCAA and HTFN’s governance and operational model, financial sustainability, and strategic positioning in order to reposition and strengthen them. The purpose of this consultancy is to conduct a comprehensive assessment and institutional appraisal of NiBUCAA and HTFN to develop actionable recommendations and a restructuring roadmap to reposition the coalition and the Trust fund for improved effectiveness, sustainability, and strategic relevance. Objectives The consultant will: Review NiBUCAA and the HTFN’s governance structure and institutional arrangements. Evaluate operational efficiency and programmatic performance since inception. Assess financial sustainability and private sector engagement mechanisms. Review policy alignment with national HIV and health financing strategies. Develop actionable recommendations and a restructuring roadmap. Scope of Work The consultant is expected to carry out the following functions: Assess Board composition, roles, and functionality. Review accountability and oversight mechanisms. Review the constitution and institutional mandate. Review organizational structure and staffing. Analyze programmatic activities Evaluate membership engagement, retention, and expansion mechanisms. Review communication and institutional visibility strategies. Assess alignment with national HIV/AIDS policies and sustainability frameworks. Deliverables 1. Inception meeting report outlining methodology and work plan. 2. Draft Assessment report detailing findings across governance, operations, finance, policy alignment, and sustainability. 3. Validated l report incorporating stakeholder feedback. 4. Roadmap with clear, actionable recommendations. 5. Dissemination meeting with stakeholders through the HTFN TWG platform in the first week of August 2026, assessment report outcome and recommendations will be shared for management decision. 5. Timeline The consultancy will be conducted between May Through August 2026, in line with the proposed timeline: S/n Activity No of Days 1. Engagement Meeting of the Technical Consultant – 1 2. Inception Desk Review – 5 3. Interviews Consultations 5 4. Data Analysis Draft Report 6 5. Validation Meeting 2 6. Dissemination during HTFN TWG Meeting 2 The duration of the consultancy is 21 working days. Qualifications and Experience The requisite qualifications of the consultant shall include the following: Degree in Social Sciences, Health Economics/Financing, or related fields, with relevant professional certifications considered an added advantage- 30 Marks Demonstrated at least 5 years’ experience working with private sector coalitions, Public sector or multi-stakeholder platforms (Supporting document with award letters, appointment letter, contract etc)- 45 Marks Excellent analytical, facilitation, and report writing skills. Evidence and Experience in institutional assessments will be an added advantage- 10 Marks Detailed work plan and Methodology- 15 Marks. Strong understanding of Nigeria’s HIV response architecture and domestic resource mobilization landscape to be provided in the methodology. Scoring Criteria for Evaluation of consultant SN Criteria Scores 1 Qualification 30 2 Experience/Understanding 55 3 Work plan (5 Marks) and Methodology (10 Marks) 15 4 Total 100 5 Minimum Score to

The male-to-female ratio for confirmed cases is 1:0.9 (Figure 4). • The number of suspected and confirmed cases increased compared to that reported for the same period in 2025. • No new healthcare worker was affected in the reporting week 20. • National Lassa fever multi-partner, multi-sectoral Incident Management System (IMS) activated to support the coordination of response activities at all levels.

title: NACA Reaffirms Commitment to Staff Welfare and Preventive Healthcare
content_text: Today, the National Agency for the Control of AIDS (NACA) held an insightful staff meeting within the agency s conference room. The session was led by the Director General, Dr. Temitope Ilori, with several Directors in attendance to engage with the workforce on key welfare and health initiatives. The meeting focused on encouraging a supportive environment and ensuring every team member has the resources needed to thrive personally and professionally. Key Highlights and Discussions Prioritizing Preventive Health: A major theme of the discussion was the importance of proactive health management. Staff were reminded to seek regular medical checkups and monitor their well-being even when they feel healthy, emphasizing that prevention is a vital component of long-term wellness. A Culture of Care: Beyond physical health, staff were encouraged to look out for one another and remain strong in their faith. This call for mutual support aimed to strengthen the bond across the various departments/units of the agency. Improving Health Insurance Services: Addressing concerns regarding the current National Health Insurance Scheme (NHIS), the management acknowledged that service quality has been suboptimal. It was suggested to explore alternative health insurance providers to ensure better coverage and care for all staff. The meeting concluded with a period of prayer for the health, protection, and safety of the entire NACA family. The session served as a great source of encouragement, leaving staff feeling valued and reassured that their health and welfare remain a top priority for the agency s leadership.
source_label: NACA (National Agency for the Control of AIDS)
summary: NACA held a staff meeting to discuss staff welfare and health initiatives, including the importance of proactive health management, a culture of care, and improving health insurance services.

title: INVITATION TO TENDER
content_text: NATIONAL AGENCY FOR THE CONTROL OF AIDS (NACA) INVITATION TO TENDER Introduction The National Agency for the Control of AIDS (NACA) coordinates the National HIV/AIDS response, providing guidance to all implementing entities, coordinating and reporting on the attainment of national objectives of the response while ensuring that resources are used in the most efficient manner. NACA’s mandate is to ensure efficient and effective coordination of the national response leading ultimately to the attainment of universal access to HIV prevention, treatment, care and support services for all Nigerians. The National Agency for the Control of AIDS (NACA), in collaboration with the Behavior Change Accelerator (BCA) is seeking a qualified private sector organisation to support the upgrade and technology enhancement of the National Call Centre on HIV/AIDS and Related Diseases (NCCH). The NCCH was officially commissioned in June 2012 to receive calls, provide information and link clients to service delivery points for HIV/AIDS, and other related diseases in Nigeria. The Nigeria Communications Commission (NCC) assigned a short code ‘6222’ to the NCCH to facilitate seamless and sustainable access across all networks in the country. The NCCH has remained a viable channel through which information on HIV/AIDS and related diseases is disseminated to callers in a timely and efficient manner. Over the years, the centre receives an average of 7,924 calls monthly, prevention and treatment interactions 62%, HIV-related consultations 30% and enquiries on other related diseases 8%. The centre receives high volume of calls during call centre-focused demand creation activities, World AIDS Day commemoration and emerging disease outbreaks. The channel of communication in the centre between callers and agents/counsellors is via inbound mobile phone calls and interactive voice response (IVR) systems in its operations. The centre is currently not equipped to make outbound calls but can only receive calls. Also, calls cannot be received by agents outside working hours (9 am – 5 pm) and during holidays except on IVR. NACA is committed to reducing new HIV infections by strengthening knowledge, awareness, and uptake of HIV prevention services across the general population, with tailored messaging for key and vulnerable groups. To advance this goal, Federal Ministry of Health and NACA seek to collaborate with private sector organisation to modernize the NCCH to improve accessibility, responsiveness, and effectiveness in meeting evolving client needs. Toward the modernization of NCCH, the Agency intends to procure the items in the table below, interested firms are therefore invited to bid accordingly: SN Description of the Item Specification Qty Frq. 1 Replacement of server hardwares. Cloud based server for standard SIP/VoIP, AWS Host, Static Public IP,8 Cores Vcpu, 16 GB RAM,40GB Storage, 3CX installed, Linux operating system, SIP integrated 1 1 2 Purchase of Inverter / Solar System Installation Hybrid Three-PHASE 20KVA /16kw-20Kw) Inverter system, 26 panels (550W each with installation accessories) Output: 230V @ 50/60Hz, 60KW/h Lithium Battery, Earthing 1 1 3 Procurement of Laptops with telephony tools for NCCH. Processor: Intel Core i7 Ultra, 16GB Ram, Full Duplex support sound card (allows for simultaneous talking and listening), Storage:500SSD, Webcam, SIP supported, CRM integration, 3CX integrated, Graphics Card Headset 3 1 4 Procurement of mobile devices for remote call centre operations. Newer Android IOS, AER, High cancelling headset, Network 5G, Battery 5,000 mAh, 256GB Storage, 12GB Ram, Octane-core chipset processor, VOIP Call supported 12 1 5 Annual call cards for agents. title: ENGAGEMENT OF CONSULTANCY FIRM FOR THE UPGRADE, EXPANSION AND TECHNOLOGY ENHANCEMENT OF SERVICES AT THE NATIONAL CALL CENTRE ON HIV/AIDS AND RELATED DISEASES (NCCH)
content_text: NATIONAL AGENCY FOR THE CONTROL OF AIDS (NACA) REQUEST FOR PROPOSAL ENGAGEMENT OF CONSULTANCY FIRM FOR THE UPGRADE, EXPANSION AND TECHNOLOGY ENHANCEMENT OF SERVICES AT THE NATIONAL CALL CENTRE ON HIV/AIDS AND RELATED DISEASES (NCCH) Introduction The National Agency for the Control of AIDS (NACA), in collaboration with the Business for Health Access (BHA) is seeking a qualified private sector organisation to support the upgrade and technology enhancement of the National Call Centre on HIV/AIDS and Related Diseases (NCCH). The NCCH was officially commissioned in June 2012 to receive calls, provide information and link clients to service delivery points for HIV/AIDS, and other related diseases in Nigeria. The Nigeria Communications Commission (NCC) assigned a short code ‘6222’ to the NCCH to facilitate seamless and sustainable access across all networks in the country. The NCCH has remained a viable channel through which information on HIV/AIDS and related diseases is disseminated to callers in a timely and efficient manner. Over the years, the centre receives an average of 7,924 calls monthly, prevention and treatment interactions 62%, HIV-related consultations 30% and enquiries on other related diseases 8%. The centre receives high volume of calls during call centre-focused demand creation activities, World AIDS Day commemoration and emerging disease outbreaks. The channel of communication in the centre between callers and agents/counsellors is via inbound mobile phone calls and interactive voice response (IVR) systems in its operations. The centre is currently not equipped to make outbound calls but can only receive calls. Also, calls cannot be received by agents outside working hours (9 am – 5 pm) and during holidays except on IVR. NACA is committed to reducing new HIV infections by strengthening knowledge, awareness, and uptake of HIV prevention services across the general population, with tailored messaging for key and vulnerable groups. To advance this goal, Federal Ministry of Health and NACA seek to collaborate with private sector organisation to modernize the NCCH to improve accessibility, responsiveness, and effectiveness in meeting evolving client needs. This call for proposals focuses on the project’s first year (2026), which will test the operational viability and potential health impact of a strengthened NCCH–private sector partnership model before broader scale-up. In 2026, the NCCH modernization activity will prioritize improving the conversion pathway from call inquiry to confirmed service uptake for: HIV Testing Services (HTS) Oral PrEP New prevention technologies, including CAB-LA and Lenacapavir One of the objectives of the NCCH upgrade is to establish and measure a functioning call-to-service within a defined geography or priority population segment. Through this public-private partnership, NACA and BHA aim to leverage private sector expertise in telecommunications, digital technology, customer experience, behavioral science, and service delivery to strengthen the NCCH as a modern, efficient, and client-centered national platform. This RFP invites qualified private sector organizations to submit proposals outlining their technical and operational expertise to support the upgrade, enhancement, and sustainability of the NCCH. Objective of the RFP The objective of this RFP is to identify and engage private sector partner(s) with the technical expertise, infrastructure, and innovation capacity to support: Outbound call activation and follow-up systems CRM deployment for referral tracking Improvement of service quality, accessibility, and responsiveness AI tools focused on triage and FAQ automation Dashboard development for performance monitoring Conversion optimization Capacity building for call centre staff Improve clients’ access to NCCH services through intelligent call routing and AI-powered virtual agents. Year 1 will focus on: Selected high-burden states or priority urban areas A defined high-risk population segment Scope of Work The selected private sector partner(s) will work closely with NACA, BHA, and relevant stakeholders to support the following key areas: Technology Upgrades with Direct Service Impact Activate outbound calling capacity Follow-up calls for referred clients Missed-call protocols Deploy CRM system for: Risk assessment recording Referral issuance tracking Follow-up documentation Service uptake verification Implement limited AI integration focused on: Risk assessment triage support FAQ automation (to reduce routine burden) Referral tracking analytics The partner will help operationalize and measure: Calls received Risk assessed Referral issued Follow-up conducted Service uptake confirmed and Institute a metric tracking for monitoring the performance of the NCCH. Interoperability Proposed solutions should prioritize interoperability and use of open standards where feasible. Any custom-developed tools, configurations, workflows, or dashboards developed specifically under this engagement shall be documented and made available to NACA for future replication and scale. Where feasible, partners are encouraged to align with principles of open digital ecosystems and scalable digital public infrastructure. Expected Deliverables The selected partner will be expected to deliver: Pilot implementation of enhanced NCCH services, including extended service hours and improved accessibility, within a defined priority geography or population segment. To include the following: System design and implementation plan CRM configured for referral and outcome tracking AI-supported triage/FAQ tool Outbound follow-up system activated Live performance dashboard Training sessions for call center agents and supervisors Baseline assessment report outlining the call center s current state and recommended upgrades to be implemented within the specified timeframe Periodic progress reports Identify opportunities to link the NCCH platform to existing or planned communication and outr

title: ENGAGEMENT OF INDIVIDUAL CONSULTANT TO DEVELOP DIGITAL VERSION OF NATIONAL PREVENTION TRAINING PACKAGE FOR COMMUNITY HIV SERVICES PROVIDERS ON ADOLESCENT GIRLS AND YOUNG WOMEN (AGYW) AND MALE SEXUAL PARTNERS PROGRAMMING.
content_text: NATIONAL AGENCY FOR THE CONTROL OF AIDS (NACA) EXPRESSION OF INTEREST ENGAGEMENT OF INDIVIDUAL CONSULTANT TO DEVELOP DIGITAL VERSION OF NATIONAL PREVENTION TRAINING PACKAGE FOR COMMUNITY HIV SERVICES PROVIDERS ON ADOLESCENT GIRLS AND YOUNG WOMEN (AGYW) AND MALE SEXUAL PARTNERS PROGRAMMING. 20 th April, 2026 INTRODUCTION Adolescents and Young People (AYP) aged 10-24 make up one-third of Nigeria’s population (Federal Ministry of Health, Nigeria). The 2018 Nigeria AIDS Indicator and Impact Survey (NAIIS) estimated that approximately 180,000 AYP are living with HIV in Nigeria, with an exceptionally high prevalence among females aged 20-24 at 1.3% (95% CI: 1.1-1.5). The survey also revealed that awareness of HIV status was lowest among AYP, with only 29.4% of those aged 20-24 and 35.5% of those aged 15-19 aware of their status, compared to 47% among older adults. Developing targeted interventions and training packages is essential for addressing their unique needs and improving outcomes. A National Prevention Training Package is proposed to equip community HIV service providers with the skills and knowledge necessary to effectively program for AGYW and their male partners. This package will also align with the National AYP Strategy on engaging HIV service providers with AGYW. To achieve the foregoing, NACA is seeking to engage a consultant for the Development of a digital version of the National Prevention Training Package and for Community HIV Services Providers on Adolescent Girls and Young Women (AGYW) and Male Sexual Partners Programming. Pre-qualification requirement To qualify for consideration, interested consultants are required to submit CVs including the following documents: Cover Letter Recent copy of CV Copies of all qualification certificates indicated in the CV Relevant experiences and supporting documents Consultant profile. TERMS OF REFERENCE (TOR) ENGAGEMENT OF INDIVIDUAL CONSULTANT TO DEVELOP DIGITAL VERSION OF NATIONAL PREVENTION TRAINING PACKAGE FOR COMMUNITY HIV SERVICES PROVIDERS ON ADOLESCENT GIRLS AND YOUNG WOMEN (AGYW) AND MALE SEXUAL PARTNERS PROGRAMMING INTRODUCTION Adolescents and Young People (AYP) aged 10-24 make up one-third of Nigeria’s population (Federal Ministry of Health, Nigeria). The 2018 Nigeria AIDS Indicator and Impact Survey (NAIIS) estimated that approximately 180,000 AYP are living with HIV in Nigeria, with an exceptionally high prevalence among females aged 20-24 at 1.3% (95% CI: 1.1-1.5). The survey also revealed that awareness of HIV status was lowest among AYP, with only 29.4% of those aged 20-24 and 35.5% of those aged 15-19 aware of their status, compared to 47% among older adults. Developing targeted interventions and training packages is essential for addressing their unique needs and improving outcomes. A National Prevention Training Package is proposed to equip community HIV service providers with the skills and knowledge necessary to effectively program for AGYW and their male partners. This package will also align with the National AYP Strategy on engaging HIV service providers with AGYW. Such efforts will foster greater community engagement and ultimately lead to improved health and well-being outcomes for AYP. Additionally, to enhance accessibility, broaden reach, provide real-time updates, encourage interactivity, and ensure sustainability, a digital version is also proposed. The NACA E-learning platform offers a sustainable system that promotes continuous knowledge transfer and eliminates the costs associated with physical training. The digital version of the prevention training package will be integrated as a module in this existing E-learning platform. OBJECTIVES OF CONSULTANCY FOR: The Development of a Digital Version of the National Prevention Training Package Consultancy To develop a digital version of the training package that enhances the capacity of community HIV service providers to deliver holistic, culturally sensitive, and effective HIV prevention interventions for AGYW and male sexual partners SCOPE OF WORK The consultant will work closely with the Community Prevention division in NACA to accomplish the following tasks: Developing a digital version of the national prevention training package. QUALIFICATIONS AND EXPERIENCE OF THE CONSULTANT This assignment requires the services of a consultant who can demonstrate skills relating to analytical capacity, especially qualitative analysis. The following qualifications and experiences are required: The digital version consultancy At least a master’s degree in public health or social sciences, especially public policy, gender studies Minimum of 3 years’ experience in creating multimedia formats (e.g., videos, interactive simulations) and adaptive learning tools to accommodate diverse learning styles for adolescents and young people. (evidence of similar job done shall be provided by submitting an award letter or contract agreement) DELIVERABLES The digital version consultancy Creation of short videos for Twitter, YouTube, Instagram, and Facebook. Conversion of all training materials into engaging digital formats (e.g., interactive PDFs, e-learning modules, and infographics). DURATION OF CONSULTANCY The consultant is responsible for developing the digital version of the National Prevention Training Package for Community HIV Service Providers on Adolescent Girls and Young Women (AGYW) and Male Sexual Partners Programming, and shall work for 20 days to deliver the entire process. REMUNERATION He/she will be paid competitive fees for the duration of the consultancy, based on the current rate for consultants, depending on qualifications and experience. The National Agency for the Control of AIDS (NACA) will make available the necessary documents and reports, and other information concerning this assignment. You may obtain additional information at the following address: National Agency for the Control of AIDS (NACA), Ground Floor, No. 3 Zinguinchor Street, Beside AEDC Office, Wuse zo

title: Engagement of Consultant for the Assessment and Appraisal of the Nigerian Business Coalition Against AIDS (NiBUCAA) and the HIV Trust Fund Nigeria (HTFN)
content_text: NATIONAL AGENCY FOR THE CONTROL OF AIDS (NACA) REQUEST FOR PROPOSAL Engagement of Consultant for the Assessment and Appraisal of the Nigerian Business Coalition Against AIDS (NiBUCAA) and the HIV Trust Fund Nigeria (HTFN) Background and Context The Nigerian Business Coalition Against AIDS (NiBUCAA) and HIV Trust Fund of Nigeria was established as a private sector platform to mobilise resources, coordinate workplace and community-based HIV prevention, care, and support interventions in Nigeria. The platforms was designed to complement the efforts of the National Agency for the Control of AIDS (NACA) by mobilizing corporate leadership, resources, and advocacy support for the national HIV response. Over time, NiBUCAA and HTFN’soperational effectiveness, private sector engagement, and institutional visibility have declined. With Nigeria’s increasing focus on domestic resource mobilization and sustainability of the HIV response, there is a critical need to assess NiBUCAA and HTFN’s governance and operational model, financial sustainability, and strategic positioning in order to reposition and strengthen them. The purpose of this consultancy is to conduct a comprehensive assessment and institutional appraisal of NiBUCAA and HTFN to develop actionable recommendations and a restructuring roadmap to reposition the coalition and the Trust fund for improved effectiveness, sustainability, and strategic relevance. Objectives The consultant will: Review NiBUCAA and the HTFN’s governance structure and institutional arrangements. Evaluate operational efficiency and programmatic performance since inception. Assess financial sustainability and private sector engagement mechanisms. Review policy alignment with national HIV and health financing strategies. Develop actionable recommendations and a restructuring roadmap. Scope of Work The consultant is expected to carry out the following functions: Assess Board composition, roles, and functionality. Review accountability and oversight mechanisms. Review the constitution and institutional mandate. Review organizational structure and staffing. Analyze programmatic activities Evaluate membership engagement, retention, and expansion mechanisms. Review communication and institutional visibility strategies.Assess alignment with national HIV/AIDS policies and sustainability frameworks. 3. Deliverables Inception meeting report outlining methodology and work plan. Draft Assessment report detailing findings across governance, operations, finance, policy alignment, and sustainability. Validated l report incorporating stakeholder feedback. Roadmap with clear, actionable recommendations. Dissemination meeting with stakeholders through the HTFN TWG platform in the second week of June 2026, assessment report outcome and recommendations will be shared for management decision 4. Timeline The consultancy will be conducted between April to June 2026, in line with the proposed timeline: S/n Activity No of Days 1. Engagement Meeting of the Technical Consultant – 1 2. Inception Desk Review – 5 3. Interviews Consultations 5 4. Data Analysis Draft Report 6 5. Validation Meeting 2 6. Dissemination during HTFN TWG Meeting 2 The duration of the consultancy is 21 working days. Demonstrated experience working with private sector coalitions or multi-stakeholder platforms. Excellent analytical, facilitation, and report writing skills. Detailed work plan and Methodology- 10 Marks Scoring Criteria for Evaluation of consultant SN Criteria Scores 1 Qualification 30 2 Experience/Understanding 60 3 Work plan (5 Marks) and Methodology (5 Marks) 10 4 Total 100 5 Minimum Score to be qualified 70 Reporting and Oversight The consultant will report to the Director, Performance Management Resource Mobilization at NACA or her designated representative. title: ENGAGEMENT OF A CONSULTANT FOR KEY POPULATION SIZE ESTIMATE DATA TRIANGULATION IN NIGERIA
content_text: NATIONAL AGENCY FOR THE CONTROL OF AIDS (NACA) REQUEST FOR PROPOSAL ENGAGEMENT OF A CONSULTANT FOR KEY POPULATION SIZE ESTIMATE DATA TRIANGULATION IN NIGERIA March, 2026 Background and Context Nigeria continues to make significant progress in its HIV response; however, Key Populations (KPs) including Female Sex Workers (FSW), Men who have Sex with Men (MSM), People Who Inject Drugs (PWID), and Transgender (TG) persons remain disproportionately affected by HIV. Effective planning, equitable resource allocation, realistic target setting, and program performance monitoring depend heavily on accurate and reliable estimates of the sizes of these populations. Over the years, Nigeria has conducted multiple KP Size Estimation (KPSE) studies using methodologies such as Programmatic Mapping, Capture–Recapture, Network Scale-Up Method (NSUM), and other multiplier approaches. While these efforts have generated valuable data, the use of different methodologies has resulted in varying and sometimes inconsistent estimates at national and sub-national levels. These discrepancies create challenges for national reporting, program planning, donor alignment, and service coverage assessment. To strengthen the evidence base for decision-making, a structured triangulation and harmonization process is required. The triangulation exercise will consolidate existing KPSE data using recognized analytical approaches (including the Anchored Multiplier Calculator – AMC) to generate unified and credible national and sub-national size estimates for use across Nigeria’s HIV response. Rationale for the Assignment The availability of multiple KP size estimates has created inconsistencies in planning assumptions, resource allocation, and performance reporting across stakeholders. Without harmonized estimates: HIV program coverage cannot be accurately measured. Targets may be overestimated or underestimated. Funding allocation may not reflect true service needs. National and donor reporting may lack consistency. The triangulation will support the National Agency for the Control of AIDS (NACA), policymakers, donors, and program implementers to make evidence-based decisions, optimize resource allocation, and improve the effectiveness of HIV interventions for key populations. Scope of Work and Detailed Tasks The consultant will be engaged for 12 work days, structured as follows: Phase 1: Desk Review and Data Extraction (4 Work Days) Tasks: Review all available KPSE reports, datasets, and technical documents. Extract relevant state and national estimates for FSW, MSM, PWID, and TG populations. Assess methodologies used, assumptions, limitations, and overlaps. Identify data gaps and inconsistencies. Prepare a summary matrix of all available estimates by state, population group, and methodology. Develop workshop materials, triangulation templates, and AMC input sheets. Deliverable of Phase 1: Inception brief summarizing review findings and triangulation plan. Consolidated KPSE data extraction matrix. Draft workshop agenda and facilitation materials. Phase 2: Facilitation of KPSE Triangulation Workshop (4 Working Days) Tasks: Facilitate a national technical triangulation workshop. Present findings from desk review and data synthesis. Guide stakeholders through application of the Anchored Multiplier Calculator (AMC) and other triangulation approaches. Lead technical discussions to reconcile differences in estimates. Document assumptions, adjustments, and consensus decisions. Support validation of harmonized estimates. Deliverable of Phase 2: Harmonized state and national KP size estimates (draft). Workshop proceedings report (assumptions, consensus decisions). Updated triangulation datasets. Phase 3: Report Writing and Final Product Development (4 Working Days) Tasks: Analyze workshop outputs and finalize triangulated estimates. Prepare comprehensive technical report detailing methodology, assumptions, results, and limitations. Provide recommendations for use of unified KPSE data in planning and reporting. Develop summary briefs and data tables for programmatic use. Incorporate stakeholder feedback and finalize report. Deliverable of Phase 3: Final KPSE Triangulation Technical Report. Clean harmonized dataset (Excel format). Executive summary brief (5–7 pages). Presentation slides for dissemination. Required Qualifications and Experience Advanced degree (Master’s or PhD) in Epidemiology, Biostatistics, Public Health, Demography, or related field. 30 MARKS At least 7–10 years of experience in HIV surveillance, epidemiology, or population size estimation.- 30 Marks Demonstrated expertise with evidence (publication, journal or any supporting document) in KP size estimation methodologies (e.g., mapping, capture–recapture, multiplier methods, NSUM). 10 Marks Proven experience using triangulation tools (Provide evidence on tools used)-10 Marks Detailed workplan and Methodology- 20 Marks Experience working with national HIV programs, preferably in Nigeria or similar contexts. Strong analytical and quantitative data management skills. Scoring Criteria for Evaluation of consultant SN Criteria Scores 1 Qualification 30 2 Experience/Understanding 50 3 Work plan (10 Marks) and Methodology (10 Marks) 20 4 Total 100 5 Minimum Score to be qualified 70 Payment Terms The consultant will be paid a lump sum professional fee covering the full 12 work days of consultancy services upon submission of all agreed deliverables. Payment shall be subject to satisfactory performance and acceptance of deliverables in accordance with the terms of this engagement. Ethical Considerations and Data Confidentiality The consultant shall: Adhere strictly to ethical standards for handling sensitive key population data. Maintain confidentiality of all datasets and documents accessed. Use data solely for the purpose of this assignment. Ensure no unauthorized sharing of state-level or population-specific estimates. Apply data protection principles in line with national and

title: ENGAGEMENT OF TWO CONSULTANTS TO CONDUCT AN EVALUATION OF HARM REDUCTION PROGRAM IN NIGERIA
content_text: NATIONAL AGENCY FOR THE CONTROL OF AIDS (NACA) REQUEST FOR PROPOSAL (RFP) ENGAGEMENT OF TWO CONSULTANTS TO CONDUCT AN EVALUATION OF HARM REDUCTION PROGRAM IN NIGERIA 16 th March, 2026 Background Harm reduction programs aim to reduce the negative consequence of drug use without necessarily requiring user to stop using drugs. In Nigeria, these programs are crucial in addressing the growing public health challenges associated with drug abuse. Nigeria has a significant population of people who inject drugs with estimates suggesting that 20% of people who use drugs are injecting drugs. The country also faces high rates of HIV and AIDS, Hepatitis B, and C while strategies, such as needle and syringe programs (NSPs) and opioid substitution therapy (OST), have been shown to be effective in reducing the transmission of these diseases. Injecting drug use is a major driver of HIV and viral hepatitis in Nigeria, with PWID facing a 28 times higher risk of HIV infection compared to the general population. While the Nigerian government approved core harm reduction services (NSP, OAT, naloxone) in 2019, implementation has been hampered by punitive drug laws, criminalization, low domestic funding, and limited-service coverage (primarily funded by Global Fund/PEPFAR). Rationale Harm reduction programs in Nigeria, there is a need to evaluate their effectiveness and identify areas for improvement. This evaluation will provide insight into the current state of harm reduction services identify gaps and inform policy and programming decision. Hence, the need to conduct the Harm reduction evaluation program in eleven (11) states in Nigeria. To achieve the foregoing, NACA is seeking to engage two 2 consultants to conduct an Evaluation of Harm Reduction Program in Nigeria. Pre-qualification requirement To qualify for consideration, interested consultants are required to submit CVs including the under listed documents below: Cover Letter Recent copy of CV Copies of all qualifications certificate Relevant experiences and supporting documents Financial Proposal TERMS OF REFERENCE (TOR) BACKGROUND Harm reduction programs aim to reduce the negative consequence of drug use without necessarily requiring user to stop using drugs. In Nigeria, these programs are crucial in addressing the growing public health challenges associated with drug abuse. Nigeria has a significant population of people who inject drugs with estimates suggesting that 20% of people who use drugs are injecting drugs. The country also faces high rates of HIV and AIDS, Hepatitis B, and C while strategies, such as needle and syringe programs (NSPs) and opioid substitution therapy (OST), have been shown to be effective in reducing the transmission of these diseases. Injecting drug use is a major driver of HIV and viral hepatitis in Nigeria, with PWID facing a 28 times higher risk of HIV infection compared to the general population. While the Nigerian government approved core harm reduction services (NSP, OAT, naloxone) in 2019, implementation has been hampered by punitive drug laws, criminalization, low domestic funding, and limited-service coverage (primarily funded by Global Fund/PEPFAR). RATIONALE Harm reduction programs in Nigeria, there is a need to evaluate their effectiveness and identify areas for improvement. This evaluation will provide insight into the current state of harm reduction services identify gaps and inform policy and programming decision. Hence, the need to conduct the Harm reduction evaluation program in eleven (11) states in Nigeria. PURPOSE The primary purpose is to assess the efficiency, effectiveness, and impact of the harm reduction services to guide national scaling-up. 4. OBJECTIVES To assesses availability of harm reduction services in Nigeria To evaluate the coverage and accessibility of NSP, OAT, and naloxone for PWID. To measure the impact of harm reduction services on reducing HIV/Hepatitis prevalence and safe injecting behaviors. To identify barriers to accessing harm reduction services and recommend strategies for improvement. To examine the sustainability of services, including domestic funding, staffing, and community ownership. METHODOLOGY The evaluation will employ a mixed-methods approach, combining quantitative and qualitative data collection methods. This will include: Quantitative Data: Analysis of service data (number of syringes distributed, number of clients in OAT, HIV testing uptake) and surveys of PWID to determine needle-sharing reduction. Qualitative Data: Key Informant Interviews (KII) with program managers, law enforcement officers, and policymakers. Focus Group Discussions (FGD) with PWID, particularly women and peer educators. Evaluation Study Area Primary data collection for the evaluation will be done in 11 states. The states are FCT, Nasarawa, Gombe, Akwa-Ibom, Cross River, Rivers, Kwara, Lagos, Ebonyi, Anambra and Abia, EVALUATION PHASES Evaluation will be conducted in three (3) phases as stated below: Inception phase: Engagement of Consultants Protocol and Tools development workshop Ethical Approval of Protocol Uploading of Tools on Kobo Collect Training of Data Collectors. Data collection phase: This includes field work for data collection and monitoring, the data collection will be carried out using the approved survey instruments, the data collection field work will be carried out by NACA staff in two batches, batch A for six states and batch B for the remaining five states. Electronic data collection application such as Kobo Collect will be used for data collection. The Questionnaires, KII and FGD tools would be embedded on the electronic Kobo Collect platform, which the data collectors will use via smartphones to conduct the interviews and record responses. The use of electronic data collection will ensure that data quality is assured. The flow of questions will be automated with necessary skip patterns, many other qualities control measures will be made possible with electronic data colle

title: ENGAGEMENT OF A CONSULTANT FOR ANNUAL HIV PROGRAM PERFORMANCE REVIEW IN THE HEALTH NON-HEALTH SECTORS
content_text: FEDERAL REPUBLIC OF NIGERIA ENGAGEMENT OF A CONSULTANT FOR ANNUAL HIV PROGRAM PERFORMANCE REVIEW IN THE HEALTH NON-HEALTH SECTORS Background and Context The National HIV Response aims to address the HIV epidemic through a multi-sectoral approach, recognizing that the response extends beyond the health sector. The non-health sector plays a critical role in addressing the social, economic, and structural determinants of HIV, including stigma, discrimination, poverty, and gender and human rights issues. To ensure the program s effectiveness, there is a need to review the current implementation of HIV interventions in both the health and the non-health sector, identify gaps, and recommend strategies for strengthening collaboration and impact. The National Agency for the Control of AIDS (NACA) seeks to engage a National Consultant to conduct a comprehensive review of the National Program on HIV in the non-health sector and provide actionable recommendations for improvement. Objectives of the Assignment The primary objective of this assignment is to review the implementation of the National Program on HIV in the health non-health sectors and provide recommendations to enhance its effectiveness. Specific objectives include: The review aims to conduct a comprehensive assessment of non-health sector HIV program progress, service coverage, and alignment with national targets for 2024 as contained in the National Strategic Plan To discuss progress made by various stakeholders in implementing the non-health sector HIV programs across the 36+1 states To identify gaps, challenges, and sustainable good practices in the program implementation To assess the level of policy implementation, the impact of policy changes, and new interventions Evaluate the level of coordination and collaboration between the health sector and non-health sectors in addressing HIV. Provide evidence-based recommendations to strengthen the program s impact, sustainability, and alignment with national and international HIV targets. title: The Resistance Fighters: Nigeria’s Bold Strategy to Outsmart a Shape-Shifting Global Threat.
content_text: The war against malaria has entered a critical new phase, and the frontlines are located in the clinics of Enugu and Kwara states,. For decades, the global health community has relied on artemisinin-based combination therapies (ACTs) as the gold standard of care, but a silent, shape-shifting enemy is emerging: antimalarial drug resistance. To counter this, Nigeria officially launched the STOP-AMDR (Scaling the Optimal Use of Multiple ACTs to Prevent Antimalarial Drug Resistance) project in October 2025, a bold initiative introduced by the Coordinating Minister of Health and Social Welfare, Professor Muhammad Ali Pate,. The project’s strategy is as scientifically rigorous as it is logistically innovative. Through a Multiple First-Line Therapy (MFT) approach, health systems simultaneously deploy different ACTs across a population. This diversification effectively reduces the selective pressure on any single medication, making it far more difficult for the malaria parasite to develop resistance,. Funded by Unitaid and led by the NMEP, the pilot is being implemented by Jhpiego in collaboration with researchers from the Universities of Calabar and Ilorin, and is overseen by a National Research Advisory Committee chaired by the World Health Organization (WHO),. Beyond the laboratory, STOP-AMDR is a massive exercise in health systems engineering. The project is currently gathering data on the feasibility and cost-effectiveness of MFT, while also strengthening the supply chains and quality assurance systems needed to sustain it,. By positioning Nigeria as a regional leader in the fight against resistance, the initiative aims to ensure that the medicines we rely on today remain both affordable and effective for the children of tomorrow,. In the corridors of the Ministry of Health, the message is clear: we must stay one step ahead of the parasite to secure a malaria-free future.
summary: The National Malaria Elimination Programme (NMEP) has launched the STOP-AMDR project to combat antimalarial drug resistance. The project aims to deploy different artemisinin-based combination therapies (ACTs) across a population to reduce the selective pressure on any single medication.

title: The 2026 Offensive: Engineering a Malaria-Free Future
content_text: As Nigeria approaches the 2026 operational year, the NMEP is preparing to launch a sophisticated, multi-front offensive that combines scientific rigor with logistical innovation. The Case Management branch will prioritize Therapeutic Efficacy Studies to monitor drug performance while scaling Seasonal Malaria Chemoprevention (SMC) to 21 eligible states. Simultaneously, the Programme Management branch is finalizing the National Malaria Strategic Plan (NMSP) 2026-2030, a blueprint that will set the definitive direction for the nation s health security for the remainder of the decade. The 2026 agenda is characterized by a precision medicine approach, deploying tools like the digital platform scale-up in Kwara and continuous net distribution pilots in Osun. From the Surveillance branch’s bi-annual data deep dives to the Procurement branch’s implementation of consumption-based forecasting, every moving part is designed for synergy. This concerted approach represents an important step in actualizing the mission of a malaria-free Nigeria, where strategic planning and community engagement meet at the frontlines.
summary: The NMEP is preparing to launch a multi-front offensive against malaria, combining scientific rigor with logistical innovation. The plan includes Therapeutic Efficacy Studies, Seasonal Malaria Chemoprevention (SMC) in 21 eligible states, and a precision medicine approach.

title: The Digital Fever: How a Tweet-Chat Reached 11 Million Souls
content_text: For the NMEP, 2025 was the year the digital barrier was finally broken. No longer content with traditional broadcast methods, the programme leveraged its social media platforms to create a space for influence, awareness, and public accountability, recording 61,696 interactions throughout the year. This digital ecosystem didn t just disseminate information; it invited Nigerians into the elimination mission, turning every tweet and post into a tool for national advocacy. The data suggests that a growing, engaged online community is becoming a vital asset in the war against the mosquito. The breakthrough moment arrived on April 25, 2025. A high-speed, live tweet-chat for World Malaria Day bypassed traditional media reach to trend nationally and touch the lives of an estimated 11 million people. The metrics surpassed all previous malaria-related digital trends, signaling a historic momentum in public interest. Beyond the raw numbers, the engagement fostered a rare sense of unity among health professionals and the general public, proving that people-centered communication can drive collective action. As the NMEP looks forward, its digital voice remains a strategic cornerstone for mobilizing communities and driving the behavioral shifts necessary to finally end malaria in Nigeria.
summary: The NMEP reports a successful digital campaign for World Malaria Day, reaching an estimated 11 million people and surpassing previous malaria-related digital trends.

title: The Human Algorithm: Why Listening is Africa’s Newest Medical Breakthrough
content_text: The question was deceptively simple: why do some life-saving health services reach their target while others fall short? To find the answer, the IMPACT project embarked on an ambitious journey of listening first, utilizing formative research across 21 states to peel back the layers of community resistance and acceptance. The findings were a revelation, painting a vivid picture of the social, cultural, and psychological barriers that dictate the uptake of malaria, immunization, and RMNCAH+N services. It became clear that for a strategy to be effective, it must respond to the local nuances of lived experience rather than follow a generic, top-down mandate. In a high-level meeting of government agencies, NGOs, and researchers, these insights were translated into an architectural blueprint for future health interventions. The dialogue moved beyond dry metrics into SBC ideation sessions, focusing on how evidence can guide smarter decisions, strengthen capacity at state levels, and inform a complete revision of national guidelines. The consensus among stakeholders was definitive: meaningful change must be measurable. By integrating SBC indicators into routine monitoring systems and creating standardized tools, Nigeria is ensuring that behavior change is no longer a soft science but a core, strategic pillar of the national health infrastructure.
summary: The National Malaria Elimination Programme (NMEP) discusses the importance of listening to local communities to improve health services, particularly for malaria, immunization, and RMNCAH+N services. They highlight the need for strategies to respond to local nuances and cultural barriers.

title: The Art of the Swap: How Flexible Logistics Prevented a Crisis
content_text: The 2025 operational year demonstrated the power of flexible commodity management as a tool for health system resilience. Faced with potential treatment disruptions during a U.S. Stop Work Order, the NMEP facilitated a historic cross-donor collaboration. By loaning 27,000 treatment courses from the Global Fund routine stocks to PMI-supported areas, the Programme averted a widespread treatment crisis, ensuring that the commodities were returned and accounted for once the disruption passed. This culture of strategic sharing was complemented by significant external support, most notably a donation of 3 million mRDTs from the Clinton Health Access Initiative in October 2025. This donation was vital for optimizing available stocks and ensuring that diagnostic standards were upheld even during periods of supply strain. Together, these actions illustrate how strategic collaboration and proactive stock management can protect service delivery at every level of the health system, safeguarding Nigeria s progress toward a malaria-free future.
summary: The NMEP facilitated a cross-donor collaboration to avert a treatment crisis during a U.S. Stop Work Order, loaning 27,000 treatment courses from the Global Fund routine stocks to PMI-supported areas and receiving a donation of 3 million mRDTs from the Clinton Health Access Initiative.

title: The Peer-to-Peer Revolution: Re-energizing the Frontlines
content_text: Sub-optimal supply chain performance is often rooted in complex local bottlenecks, a reality the NMEP addressed head-on in 2025 through a series of high-level advocacy visits. Conducted in collaboration with Catholic Relief Services, these visits targeted donor-supported states identified as needing improvement, such as Gombe, Adamawa, and Kano. The goal was to move beyond the data and engage in open dialogue with State Ministries of Health to solve problems in distribution and accountability. A unique aspect of this strategy was the use of Osun State as a learning site, allowing officials from other states to observe best practices in commodity logistics first-hand. This peer-learning approach encouraged the sharing of context-specific solutions and reinforced the idea that success is achievable through disciplined management. By re-energizing state leadership and aligning stakeholders around national policies, the NMEP is building a more efficient and transparent network that ensures malaria services remain uninterrupted across the entire federation.
summary: The NMEP addressed sub-optimal supply chain performance in 2025 through high-level advocacy visits to donor-supported states, including Gombe, Adamawa, and Kano, to engage in open dialogue with State Ministries of Health to solve problems in distribution and accountability.

title: The Vigilant Supply Chain: Protecting Patients from the Shadows
content_text: The integrity of Nigeria’s malaria treatment relies on a supply chain that is both resilient and transparent. Throughout 2025, the NMEP carried out a series of intensive supportive supervision visits across 21 states, auditing 188 facilities to reinforce best practices in commodity management. These visits aimed to prevent the wastage of resources through expiry and to ensure that essential medicines are always available when a fever strikes. The importance of this oversight was highlighted by a rapid-response intervention involving mislabeled S-KANT SPAQ 2 tablets. The Programme’s ability to quickly engage NAFDAC for a safety analysis ensured that dosage accuracy was maintained, protecting patient confidence in the health system. Furthermore, the NMEP successfully used letters of indemnity to prevent the expiry of artesunate injections in IMPACT-supported states, a move that saved both money and lives. These actions demonstrate a commitment to safeguarding commodity quality and ensuring that every patient receives a treatment they can trust.
summary: The NMEP conducted supportive supervision visits across 21 states to reinforce best practices in commodity management, preventing wastage of resources and ensuring essential medicines are available. They also successfully used letters of indemnity to prevent the expiry of artesunate injections in IMPACT-supported states, saving money and lives.

title: The ₦29 Billion Green Light: Cutting Red Tape to Save Lives
content_text: The logistical complexity of importing life-saving commodities can often be a silent killer in public health, but Nigeria’s 2025 initiative on import duty exemptions has set a new standard for efficiency. By processing waivers valued at over ₦29.5 billion, the NMEP has significantly reduced the cost and administrative hurdles associated with bringing essential malaria products into the country. This was achieved through a revolutionary pooling system, where all approved malaria health products across various implementing partners were consolidated into a single request. This harmonized strategy shortened wait times for critical exemptions from the Federal Ministry of Finance and electronic licenses from NAFDAC. The result is a supply chain that is not only faster but also more transparent and accountable. By reducing duplication and improving coordination, the Programme has safeguarded uninterrupted access to prevention and treatment tools nationwide. It is a powerful reminder that in the war against malaria, the paperwork behind the scenes is just as vital as the medicine in the clinic.
summary: The NMEP has implemented a new system to reduce the cost and administrative hurdles associated with importing malaria products into the country, resulting in a supply chain that is faster, more transparent, and accountable.
